text.title: Orchestrator Asserts a Real RED to GREEN Transition
text.summary: The orchestrator runs the test command between turns and aborts the cycle when RED's first run unexpectedly passes — the spec was wrong or the test is not testing it.

section.title: Cycle invariants
section.p: RED hands off only the new test file path; GREEN never sees the spec. The orchestrator is the only component that runs the test runner and is therefore the only component that knows whether the build is red or green.

rule.statement: Between RED and GREEN turns the orchestrator MUST: (1) run the project's test command on the new test, (2) require exit code != 0, (3) abort and surface "first-try-pass" if exit code == 0, (4) pass to GREEN ONLY the failing test diff and the runner's failure stanza — never the original AC, never the test source. After GREEN, the orchestrator re-runs the same command and refuses to commit unless exit code == 0.
rule.rationale: A passing first-run test means either the feature already exists or the test does not exercise the spec; either case is a hard signal to halt rather than continue. Keeping the AC out of GREEN's prompt prevents AC paraphrasing from leaking implementation hints back into the test.

example.code: ORCHESTRATOR LOG: RED -> wrote tests/test_refund.py::test_refund_within_14_days RUN -> pytest -q -> 1 failed (AssertionError) GREEN -> edited src/refund.py RUN -> pytest -q -> 1 passed COMMIT
example.note: Real RED, then real GREEN, both confirmed by the runner.

example.code: ORCHESTRATOR LOG: RED -> wrote tests/test_refund.py::test_refund_within_14_days RUN -> pytest -q -> 1 passed (!!) GREEN -> (skipped, already green) COMMIT
example.note: First-try-pass committed; the test is almost certainly tautological.

antipattern.description: Orchestrator passes the original acceptance criterion to GREEN "for context."
antipattern.reason: GREEN should derive intent from the failing test alone; reintroducing the AC lets the model satisfy the AC text without making the test pass.

section.reference: Custom TDD orchestrator with Claude Code skills (alexop.dev).
